464da9fa87b033fbc74a.text:
**Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member onboarding**: no documented flow from signup → first class → habit formation (risk: inconsistent experience, early churn)
- **Churn & win-back**: no process for pause requests, cancellations, or re-engagement (financial leakage)
- **Coaching standards & QA**: no class quality audit or coaching development roadmap (inconsistent member experience, retention risk)
- **Member check-ins & outcomes**: no cadence for progress tracking or milestone celebration (weak retention lever)
- **Referral programme**: no process to capture, incentivise, or track member-led growth
- **GDPR & data privacy**: no documented handling of member records, consent, data requests (compliance risk)
- **Complaints & incidents**: no escalation or resolution protocol (reputation & legal risk)
- **Supply ordering**: no predictable restocking for towels, cleaning, retail (operational friction)
- **New quarter program launch**: no structured rollout of class offerings, marketing, or comms (inconsistent growth planning)

---

**Needs updating**

- **Coach onboarding & training**: likely missing performance metrics, shadowing sign-off, or ongoing development checkpoints
- **Billing & payments**: probably doesn't cover pause/freeze workflows, refund disputes, or late-payment escalation
- **Member communications**: may not include retention email sequences, milestone triggers, or lapsed-member outreach
- **Facilities & cleaning**: lacks deep-clean cadence, equipment maintenance logs, or health & safety incident reporting
- **Marketing**: 5 SOPs exist but probably missing referral capture mechanics, member testimonial sourcing, or seasonal campaign planning

---

**Build next**

1. **Member Onboarding (Priority 1)**: Map signup → goal-setting → coach assignment → first 3 classes → retention check-in at

ef78fa4eea1cbb63abd4.text:
# FRACTIONAL CFO REPORT — THE FORGE, JUNE 2026

## REVENUE — INCREASE

- **Pricing drift down: AR/M collapsed from £170.43 (Dec) to £120.48 (June).** This is your single biggest red flag. Even though member count climbed to 147, revenue-per-member has fallen 29% in six months. Audit your pricing tiers immediately — are intro offers locking in too-low rates? Are lapsed members re-joining at discount? Raise base membership by £5–8/month on new sign-ups and first renewal cohorts; grandfather existing members for 90 days then notify.
- **December grossed £21,134 on 124 members (£170/m); June grossed £17,711 on 147 members (£120/m).** You've added 23 paying members but lost £3,423 in absolute revenue. The new studio should be *enabling* price rises and premium offerings (PT, class bundles, nutrition coaching), not discounting to fill it. Launch a 4-week "launch membership" at regular price, end it 30 June.
- **Churn collapsed to 0% in June (vs 5.74% in May) — retention has stabilised.** Hold this line with retention comms and a 90-day win-back campaign for the 6 members lost in May. Each returning member at current rates adds ~£120/month recurring.
- **Class utilisation data missing but new studio is 2x size.** Peak-hour class capacity is likely slack. Introduce a "quiet hours" off-peak pricing (−15%), stagger classes to 8–9 per day, and add one premium 6am "power" class at +£3 premium per booking. This fills inventory without brand dilution.

---

## EXPENSES — REDUCE

- **Non-people expenses surged £1,056 month-on-month (May: £9,233 → June: £9,390) despite April relocation being complete.** Utilities, rent, or supply costs are still elevated post-move. Pull your June invoices: isolate rent/rates, insurance, utilities, and cleaning. April was £30k (relocation); post-move baseline should be ~£6–7k. If June non-people is genuinely £9,390 run-rate, that's £12,500+ over-budget annually. Target: £6,500/month by month-end via renegotiated supplier terms, LED lighting retrofit rebate, or consolidated waste/cleaning contracts.
- **People costs £3,686 (June) are up

f527670fd10b0672288e.text:
• **Redistribute Grace's classes immediately**: Her Build: Lower (17 sessions) should shift to JoJo; Elevate (6) and Strongevity (3) to Eilis. This removes Grace from the timetable and uses JoJo's strength in structured lifting and Eilis's proven Elevate experience.

• **Move Grace's PT (0 sessions going forward) and reduce her InBody scans**: Her 9 InBody scans can be handled by JoJo or Eilis in spare slots—they're admin, not coaching. This frees Grace entirely.

• **Rebalance JoJo's PT load**: At 44 PTs, JoJo is over-loaded. Shift 8–10 of these to Eilis to build her confidence and create headroom for JoJo to lead Tuesdays without burnout. Eilis currently has only 11 PTs and capacity to grow.

• **Close the No Sweat Intro gap**: Grace ran 3; these should rotate between JoJo and Eilis on a monthly rota to avoid single-person dependency and give both exposure to new-member onboarding.

• **Plug Mobility and Circuits**: JoJo covers both (7 + 6 sessions). Consider whether Eilis can lead Mobility once confident, or whether these low-frequency classes can be consolidated or guest-coached to ease JoJo's timetable.

• **Cap JoJo at ~115 sessions/month post-rebalance**: Current 133 is unsustainable for a head coach. Shift PT and Lower-body Build to Eilis, keep JoJo's Tuesday technical classes and upper-body specialism, and protect her capacity for quality delivery and team support.

c17ea6e513949b5bd341.text:
**For quiet studio time gaps:**

1. **Add a 30-min "Open Lift" slot on Monday and Thursday evenings** (your quietest days) between PT blocks — members can drop in to use equipment unsupervised or book a solo coaching session, creating revenue from dead time and giving flexible-schedule members an alternative to set classes.

2. **Run a monthly "Intro to Engine" workshop on a quiet Thursday morning** — Engine is severely underattended (15 last month); a 45-min guided intro explaining the format, pacing strategy, and why it complements Build classes could convert curious members who find the standalone class intimidating.

**To lift the quietest classes:**

3. **Move Barbelle and Strongevity to Wednesday (your busiest day) and promote them as "strength pairing" add-ons** — send targeted invites to your Build: Upper/Lower regulars 48 hours before, positioning these as complementary sessions; low attendance suggests positioning/timing issue, not demand issue.

47b0548900970eee6400.text:
# Service Menu Build — Execution for Grace

• **Start with your revenue anchor, not your offering.** Interview your top 5 paying members THIS WEEK and ask: "What's the ONE thing you'd be devastated to lose?" Your menu should lead with what's actually driving money in, not what you think should be there. Build the member menu around that.

• **Create TWO separate docs, not one.** Coach menu = what they're authorized to sell/offer + commission structure + upsell triggers (e.g., "if member asks about nutrition, coach directs to PT package"). Member menu = pricing + package lengths + what's genuinely included (be ruthless — only list what you actually deliver consistently).

• **Price by time-cost and perceived value, simultaneously.** Stack your offerings like: Base membership (unlimited classes), PT add-on (+£X/month for 2 sessions), nutrition coaching (+£Y as premium tier). The gaps between tiers should feel obvious, not arbitrary. Test one tier increase this month — if 20%+ of new members choose it, you've priced it right.

• **Use this to plug coaching knowledge gaps TODAY.** When coaches see the official menu, they'll instantly spot what they can't confidently sell. Flag those gaps immediately — it's your training priority for next month.

• **Make it a one-pager for each audience.** If it's longer than one page, you're either offering too much or haven't clarified your positioning. Post member menu in-gym and in welcome emails. Coach menu gets laminated by the desk.

What's your highest-revenue service currently — membership, PT, or something else?

e4aeda2aa0d1508a8f79.text:
**Insights**

Your cost-per-lead has climbed 350% since April (£6 to £27) whilst spend has collapsed 75% (£435 to £109). This isn't budget efficiency—it's under-investment hitting a wall. July's 4 leads is critically low; at this volume you've no statistical read on what works. Your close rate remains strong (75% in July, competitive with your Apr/May average of ~60%), so the product converts fine. The real problem: you're starving the funnel.

The trajectory shows classic ad fatigue compounded by insufficient spend to test and scale. April's £6 CPL was likely aided by fresh creative and loose targeting hitting warm audiences; by June you were burning through interest without refresh. July's dramatic spend cut suggests you reacted to rising costs, but that's made the problem worse—lower spend = smaller audience pool = more auction competition for scraps = higher CPL.

**Actions**

**Immediately restore spend to £350–400 for August.** You need volume to diagnose what's broken. At £109/month you're invisible in Meta's auction and can't A/B anything meaningful. Return to April's budget band; the profit per lead is still healthy (£189 at 75% close) even at £27 CPL, so the math works.

**Overhaul creative.** You've likely exhausted April's assets. Commission 3–4 new video angles: (1) member transformation 30-second testimonial, (2) "women only" trust/safety angle (differentiator vs. mixed gyms), (3) "beginner-friendly, zero judgment" messaging, (4) before/after or progress clips set to trending audio. Kill anything that's been live >6 weeks.

**Narrow targeting and layer in lookalike.** Test two audiences in parallel: (A) tight 25–45 Belfast women, interests in fitness/wellness/health, exclude anyone who's engaged your ads >3 times (they're expensive repeat clickers); (B) 1% lookalike seed from your April leads cohort (your best CPL month—those people had something in common). Kill broad targeting.

**Audit landing page friction.** A 75% close

fbb52f88c48e54607ffa.text:
# The Forge — Fractional CFO Brief

## Revenue Trends & Issues

**Last 3 months (Apr–Jun) vs prior 3 (Jan–Mar):**
- Revenue *down* 5.8% (£14,806 avg vs £14,507 avg) — flat, not growth
- **Rev/member collapsed from £130 to £120** — your pricing or mix is weakening
- Churn spiked to 11.9% in May post-relocation, now settled at 3.2% in June (good recovery)
- Member count grew 112→147 (+31%), but revenue didn't track it — **you're onboarding lower-value members or discounting**

**Single biggest priority: Fix your pricing/product mix immediately.**

---

## INCREASE REVENUE — *Specific Actions*

- **Audit your membership tiers NOW.** Jun shows 147 members but only £107/member rev — down from £138 in Apr. Check: (1) Are new post-relocation members on lower tier offers? (2) Are card/class packs underpriced? Target: restore to £130+/member by Q3. That's £1,910 extra monthly at current headcount.

- **Launch a upsell sprint immediately.** Current members in Jun are *not* buying add-ons (retail, PT, nutrition classes, merchandise). Segment your 147 members: identify the 40–50 most engaged, offer them a *single* premium add-on (e.g. monthly PT top-up £40–60 or retail discount bundle). Conservative take-up of 20% = £400–500/month new revenue, zero headcount cost.

- **Plug the high-churn months.** May was 11.9% churn (a post-move blip, OK), but Dec 2025 was also 11.8% — **something seasonal or structural is bleeding members in May/Dec.** Run a quick exit survey: are members citing class times, equipment, or price? Dec churn often reflects Jan/Feb cancellations from Jan joiners — likely weak onboarding. Add a week-1 check-in call for all new members; target churn back to 3–4%.

- **Fill off-peak hours.** You don't share a class timetable, but 147 members on one studio (post-relocation) likely means peak-time crowds and empty slots. Launch a "quiet class" membership tier (09:00–14:00 only, off-peak) at 20–25% discount — appeals to retirees, WFH members.
